epigraph.text: I have said these things to you, that in me you may have peace. In the world you will have tribulation. But take heart; I have overcome the world.
epigraph.quote: The theory of probability is at bottom nothing but common sense reduced to calculus.

content.text:
A random variable provides a bridge between probability and statistics, 
allowing us to use the tools of mathematics—algebra, calculus, and 
analysis—to study random phenomena.
The Concept of a Random Variable

content.title: Random Variable
content.content:
A **random variable** is a function that 
assigns a numerical value to each outcome in a sample space.

We typically denote random variables by capital letters ($X$, $Y$, $Z$) 
and their possible values by lowercase letters ($x$, $y$, $z$).

content.title: Random Variables
content.problem:
Consider flipping a coin three times. Define the random variable $X$ = 
"number of heads."
content.solution:
The sample space is $S = \{HHH, HHT, HTH, THH, HTT, THT, TTH, TTT\}$.

$X$ assigns values to outcomes:

| Outcome | $X$ (number of heads) |
| TTT | 0 |
| HTT, THT, TTH | 1 |
| HHT, HTH, THH | 2 |
| HHH | 3 |

The possible values of $X$ are $\{0, 1, 2, 3\}$.

content.title: Discrete and Continuous Random Variables
content.content:
A random variable is **discrete** if 
its possible values form a finite or countably infinite list.

A random variable is **continuous** 
if it can take any value in an interval of real numbers.

content.text:
In this chapter, we focus on discrete random variables. Continuous 
random variables are treated in Chapter 4.

The probability distribution of a discrete random variable specifies 
the probability of each possible value.

content.title: Probability Mass Function (PMF)
content.content:
The **probability mass function** 
(PMF) of a discrete random variable $X$ is the function
\[
 p(x) = P(X = x)
\]
that gives the probability that $X$ equals each value $x$.

A valid PMF must satisfy:

1. $p(x) \geq 0$ for all $x$

2. $\sum_{\text{all } x} p(x) = 1$

content.title: Finding a PMF
content.problem:
For the coin-flipping example ($X$ = number of heads in 3 flips), find 
the PMF.
content.solution:
By counting outcomes:

- $P(X = 0) = \frac{1}{8}$ (only TTT)

- $P(X = 1) = \frac{3}{8}$ (HTT, THT, TTH)

- $P(X = 2) = \frac{3}{8}$ (HHT, HTH, THH)

- $P(X = 3) = \frac{1}{8}$ (only HHH)

The PMF is:

| $x$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 |
| $p(x)$ | $\frac{1}{8}$ | $\frac{3}{8}$ | $\frac{3}{8}$ | $\frac{1}{8}$ |

Check: $\frac{1}{8} + \frac{3}{8} + \frac{3}{8} + \frac{1}{8} = 1$.

content.title: Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)
content.content:
The **cumulative distribution function** 
(CDF) of a random variable $X$ is:
\[
 F(x) = P(X \leq x)
\]
For discrete random variables: $F(x) = \sum_{t \leq x} p(t)$

marginNotes.text:
But even the hairs of your head are all 
numbered.
